stories.title: Kolter starts Delray Live Local rental project with $92M financing
stories.excerpt: Kolter Multifamily kicked off construction of its Live Local Act apartment project Alton Delray after landing $91.7 million in financing, records show. The Delray Beach-based developer scored the loan from U.S. Bank for the 386-unit multifamily development planned at 2101 South Congress Avenue in Delray Beach. Forty percent of the apartments, or 155 units, will be dedicated to workforce housing under Florida’s Live Local Act. A Kolter entity paid $21.9 million in 2024 to acquire the 12.3-acre industrial-zoned site from the George T. Elmore Trust, records show. The Live Local Act allows developers to build multifamily projects on commercial sites […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Kolter secures $91.7M financing from U.S. Bank for 386-unit Delray Beach project.
40% of units (155) reserved for workforce housing under Florida Live Local Act.
Developer pursuing 2-3 additional affordable housing projects in South Florida pipeline.
stories.summary: Kolter Multifamily broke ground on Alton Delray, a 386-unit apartment community in Delray Beach funded by a $91.7 million U.S. Bank construction loan. The project dedicates 155 units to workforce housing at or below 120% AMI ($107,600 for Palm Beach County), leveraging Florida's Live Local Act zoning exemption for commercial-to-residential conversion. First delivery is targeted for late 2027, with the developer signaling an additional 2-3 affordable projects in the South Florida pipeline.
stories.impact: Strong signal that affordable housing incentives (Live Local Act) are unlocking development on underutilized commercial land in South Florida. Kolter's stated multi-project pipeline suggests the regulatory pathway is now repeatable and fundable; watch for similar conversions across Delray/Palm Beach County that compress timelines and de-risk entitlements versus traditional rezoning. This softens supply constraints and supports workforce retention in high-cost coastal markets.

stories.title: Greek Real Estate Partners JV Seals $79M Financing Package for N.J. Industrial Dev
stories.excerpt: A joint venture led by Greek Real Estate Partners (GREP) has secured a $79 million financing package for an industrial development in Parsippany, N.J., Commercial Observer has learned. The deal clears the way for the vertical construction of a 281,215-square-foot, Class A industrial facility at 169 Lackawanna Avenue. The joint venture consisted of GREP, real estate development firm the Hampshire Companies and third-party capital, advised by Principal Asset Management. “With new supply constrained and demand for modern logistics space remaining strong, we believe the project is well positioned to deliver a high-quality facility and long-term value in a tightening market,” Christopher Imperatore, asset manager at Hampshire Companies, said in a statement. The $79 million financing package was a combination of joint venture equity and construction financing, including a $49 million recapitalization and a $30 million construction loan. The construction financing was provided by Provident Bank, while further details on the recapitalization were unclear. Cushman & Wakefield’s John Alascio, TJ Sullivan, Chuck Kohaut and Nick Scibelli sourced the joint venture equity and debt for the transaction. Leasing will be helmed by KBC Advisors’ Marc Petrella, Andrew Siemsen and Sean Kelly. “We are pleased to support an experienced ownership and development group as it advances a market-leading Class A,” Ryan Greer, vice president of commercial lending at Provident Bank, said in a statement. The Lackawanna Avenue site was acquired by GREP in 2023 as an occupied office building, but its past lives include a candy manufacturing operation and a New York Life data center. GREP’s Greek Design-Build undertook site demolition and clearing. GREP anticipates “substantial completion” in the first quarter of 2027. In addition to the 40-foot industrial facility, site plans include a 2,500-square-foot speculative office, 39 loading docks and a large parking area. “Few sites tell a story like 1
bullets: Greek Real Estate Partners JV closes $79M (equity + construction) for 281K sf industrial in Parsippany, N.J.
Provident Bank provided $30M construction loan; $49M recapitalization component.
Class A logistics facility targets tight market with constrained new supply and strong demand.
stories.summary: A joint venture of Greek Real Estate Partners, Hampshire Companies, and Principal Asset Management secured $79 million in financing (combining equity and construction debt) to build a 281,215-square-foot Class A industrial facility in Parsippany, N.J. Provident Bank provided a $30 million construction loan alongside a $49 million recapitalization. The project is positioned to capture demand from tight-supply logistics market.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily. Industrial fundamentals (supply constraint, strong demand, favorable financing environment) remain healthy but do not drive apartment demand or supply decisions. However, regional construction activity and debt availability in N.J./Northeast do provide secondary signals on broader capital availability for real estate development across asset classes.

stories.title: Law Firm Fenwick & West Signs Lease Extension, Expansion
stories.excerpt: Koeppel Rosen LLC announced that law firm Fenwick & West, LLP has expanded its office footprint at 902 Broadway with a new 18,200-square-foot lease expansion and extension. Fenwick & West originally occupied 54,600 rentable square feet across three full floors and now occupies a fourth for a total of 72,800 square feet The expansion arose from Fenwick & West’s need for additional office space as it continues growing its presence in New York City. The new lease carries a term of 11 years, which includes an extension on the existing floors. Director of leasing Max P. Koeppel of Koeppel Rosen represented ownership. David M. Kleinhandler and Ken Rapp of CBRE represented the tenant. “We are thrilled that Fenwick & West has chosen to expand its commitment to 902 Broadway,” said Koeppel. “We continue to see strong interest throughout our portfolio from law firms and other professional organizations that value premier locations, efficient floorplates, and a high-quality workplace experience.” The post Law Firm Fenwick & West Signs Lease Extension, Expansion appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Fenwick & West expands NYC office 18.2K sf at 902 Broadway; new total 72.8K sf.
11-year lease term signals long-term commitment to NYC presence.
Law firm expansion reflects strong interest from professional tenants in premium office space.
stories.summary: Law firm Fenwick & West expanded its office footprint at 902 Broadway in Manhattan, adding 18,200 square feet (from 54,600 to 72,800 total) under an 11-year lease extension and expansion. The deal underscores ongoing tenant appetite for premier office locations with efficient floor plates and modern workplace amenities.
stories.impact: Office leasing activity (especially law firms renewing/expanding) is a weak leading indicator for multifamily demand in CBD markets like NYC. However, this signals workplace stability and retained professional employment in Manhattan, which underpins rental demand for high-end apartments near office clusters. No material impact on multifamily development strategy or underwriting unless part of broader office-to-resi conversion thesis in that market.

stories.title: Farallon Partnership Trades Historic Hawthorne Hangar Campus
stories.excerpt: CBRE arranged the sale of the Hawthorne Hangar Campus at 3507 Jack Northrop Ave. in Hawthorne, a 40,553-square-foot industrial flex / R&D campus. The property holds significant aviation history as the original office and airport hangar of Jack Northrop, the pioneering aviation entrepreneur and founder of Northrop Corporation. Michael Longo and Melissa Moock of CBRE’s National Partners, in collaboration with CBRE’s Private Capital Partners, led by Anthony DeLorenzo and Mark Shaffer and CBRE’s Airport and Aviation Properties Practice Group, led by Katrin Gist represented the seller, Farallon Capital Management, Jofa Capital and Urban Investment Research Corporation (URC). “Our firm acquired the asset in 2023, consistent with our ‘Funky & Fragmented’ investment thesis,” said Jackson Brissette, managing principal at Jofa Capital. “We were pleased to secure a long-term lease with a Fortune 100 tenant.” According to law firm Polsinelli, which represented the sellers, Farallon, Jofa and URC acquired the property out of bankruptcy for $13.35 million in 2023 and now have sold it for $32.5 million. The Polsinelli team was led by shareholder Jed W. Bonner and included shareholder Emina Kwok and associate Andrew J. Peterson. Connect Industrial West | August 20 | Irvine, CAOn August 20, Connect Industrial West will bring together the owners, investors, developers, brokers, lenders, and occupiers driving industrial real estate across the Western U.S. Join top decision-makers for an afternoon of market insights, dealmaking opportunities, and networking with the industry’s most influential players. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post Farallon Partnership Trades Historic Hawthorne Hangar Campus appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Hawthorne Hangar Campus (40.5K sf industrial) sold for $32.5M after bankruptcy purchase for $13.35M in 2023.
144% appreciation in 2.5 years on aerospace/R&D flex facility near Los Angeles.
Fortune 100 tenant secured long-term lease; Jofa Capital/Farallon deployed 'Funky & Fragmented' thesis.
stories.summary: Farallon Capital, Jofa Capital, and URC acquired the historic Hawthorne Hangar Campus industrial/R&D property out of bankruptcy for $13.35 million in 2023 and sold it for $32.5 million to an undisclosed buyer with a locked Fortune 100 tenant. The 144% asset appreciation in under three years reflects strong aerospace/R&D occupier demand and tight industrial supply in Los Angeles.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily. Industrial real estate (especially aerospace/R&D flex) operates on different demand/supply dynamics than apartment housing. However, the underlying thesis—tight supply, strong occupier demand, and capital chasing scarcity—is relevant to multifamily in LA markets where industrial-adjacent mixed-use redevelopment may offer alternatives to pure apartment plays.

stories.title: Rockpoint, LCOR plot historic 299-unit resi complex in Georgetown
stories.excerpt: Washington, D.C.’s Georgetown is slated for its largest multifamily project in more than a century. Rockpoint, LCOR and Potomac Investment Properties are redeveloping 1000 and 1050 Thomas Jefferson Street NW in Georgetown through a joint venture, Multi-Housing News reported. The latter is the owner of the two properties, which span a combined 349,000 square feet, having developed them in the early 1980s. The project is expected to net 299 rental units, split between a 10-story, 179-unit building and a nine-story, 120-unit building. It will be the first multifamily project in Georgetown to exceed 150 units in more than a century, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Rockpoint/LCOR/Potomac IP redeveloping 349K sf Georgetown site into 299-unit apartment complex.
First multifamily project >150 units in Georgetown in 100+ years; 10- and 9-story mid-rises planned.
D.C. conversion pipeline: 32 adaptive reuse projects totaling 7M sf underway (vs. 4/year 2016-2024).
stories.summary: A joint venture of Rockpoint, LCOR, and Potomac Investment Properties is redeveloping two 1980s-era office/commercial properties at 1000-1050 Thomas Jefferson Street NW in Georgetown into 299 apartments across two mid-rise buildings (179 and 120 units), plus 18,000 square feet of retail. The project marks Georgetown's first >150-unit multifamily development in over a century and received Old Georgetown Board and Commission of Fine Arts approval. D.C. is in the midst of a conversion boom: 32 adaptive reuse projects totaling ~7 million square feet are in the pipeline, compared to a historical average of four conversions per year (2016-2024).
stories.impact: Conversion acceleration in Washington, D.C. signals regulatory pathway clarity and investor appetite for de-risked adaptive reuse over ground-up development. This increases supply and may temper rent growth in the near term, but the sheer volume (7M sf pipeline) also signals that office-to-resi conversions are cannibalizing demand from traditional multifamily development. Developers should monitor conversion absorption rates and tenant preferences (converted vs. new-build) to refine positioning in D.C. metro submarkets.

stories.title: West Dundee pitches 100-acre Spring Hill Mall site for redevelopment
stories.excerpt: West Dundee is courting developers for the former Spring Hill Mall site, using interactive modeling to pitch a mixed-use future for the property. Village officials and design firm Houseal Lavigne Associates presented two conceptual models this week to spur interest in the 100-acre site near Routes 31 and 72, the Daily Herald reported. West Dundee spent $13 million to acquire multiple mall parcels, including some previously held by landlord Kohan Retail Investment Group, a New York-based firm that has turned its attention to buying Chicago office buildings at steep discounts. The village last year demolished the 1980s-era shopping center to […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: West Dundee (Chicago) markets 100-acre Spring Hill Mall site for 778-1,326 housing units.
Village spent $13M acquiring mall parcels; demolished center; timeline 10+ years for redevelopment.
Conceptual models: 'Urban Village' (778 units, 320K sf retail) vs. 'Mixed-Use Boulevard' (1,326 units, 400K sf retail).
stories.summary: West Dundee is actively marketing the 100-acre former Spring Hill Mall site to developers, proposing two master-plan concepts: an "urban village" (778 units, 320,000 sf retail, 5-story max) and a "mixed-use boulevard" (1,326 units, 400,000 sf retail). The village acquired ~$13 million in mall parcels and demolished the 1980s shopping center; the full redevelopment is expected to span 10 years. Both designs emphasize pedestrian-oriented streetscapes and parks.
stories.impact: Suburban mall-to-resi conversion is accelerating across Chicagoland as municipalities take direct control. West Dundee's high unit-count targets (778–1,326) signal belief in demand depth for suburban rental housing, supported by transit and lifestyle amenities rather than density alone. The 10-year timeline is realistic but long; watch for early infrastructure deals and ground-lease structures that de-risk capital deployment. This reflects broader retail decline and multifamily's structural growth in second-tier suburbs.

stories.title: Expanding Floral Distributor Buys Facility in L.A. County
stories.excerpt: A flower importer and wholesaler is in growth mode, and has planted roots in one of Los Angeles’ industrial hubs. Holland Flower Market paid $34.1 million for a 91,010-square-foot, Class A, freestanding industrial facility in the city of Commerce, Calif. BMO Bank provided a $25 million acquisition loan, according to records on PropertyShark. Records also show the seller, Vancouver-based QuadReal Property Group, had acquired the 3.5-acre property at 5555 East Slauson Avenue for $25.8 million in June 2021, representing a roughly 32 percent increase in value in five years. Holland Flower Market serves grocery retailers across the Western U.S., and required a larger and more advanced facility to support increasing demand, operational scale and investment in automation. The facility includes six dock-high loading positions and one grade-level door, as well as 32-foot minimum clear heights, a fully secured truck court, and a 9,994-square-foot, two-story office component. Newmark’s Jeff Cannon, Sage Segal and Greg Tippin represented the buyer and announced the transaction. “This acquisition reflects a broader trend across Southern California, where owner-users are capitalizing on unique opportunities created by market dynamics,” Segal said in a statement. “Over the past 24 months, softer leasing conditions have led some institutional owners to divest assets, creating a window for growth-oriented occupiers to secure high-quality facilities in prime infill locations.” L.A.’s industrial market has a vacancy rate holding at a comparatively low 4.2 percent after leasing activity rebounded in the first quarter this year, according to Newmark’s data. The market continues to benefit from underlying demand tied to port activity and consumer spending, and a “flight-to-quality” trend has pushed occupiers to newer facilities. However, a 26 percent correction in infill rents from peak levels, along with elevated concessions, has created more favorable conditions for tenants and owner-u
bullets: Holland Flower Market pays $34.1M for 91K sf Class A industrial facility in Commerce, Calif.
BMO Bank funded $25M acquisition loan; seller QuadReal Property Group gained 32% appreciation since June 2021.
Occupier expansion driven by increased demand, operational scale, and automation investment.
stories.summary: Holland Flower Market, a Western U.S. floral importer and wholesaler, acquired a 91,010-square-foot Class A industrial facility at 5555 East Slauson Avenue in Commerce, California, for $34.1 million with a $25 million BMO Bank acquisition loan. The property, previously owned by Vancouver-based QuadReal Property Group (acquired June 2021 for $25.8 million), represents a 32% appreciation in five years. The buyer's expansion reflects growing demand for grocery distribution and need for advanced logistics infrastructure with 32-foot clear heights and automation-ready spaces.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily. However, the logistics/distribution occupier expansion in LA County underscores tight industrial supply and strong last-mile demand, which correlates with employment growth and wage dynamics in industrial-adjacent areas. Multifamily developers should track industrial employment clusters (Commerce, Vernon, SCAG-designated logistics zones) as co-location amenities for workforce housing near distribution hubs gain importance.

stories.title: Brandon Johnson floats $425M taxpayer assist for Related Midwest’s The 78
stories.excerpt: Chicago Fire owner Joe Mansueto pitched his proposed South Loop soccer stadium as a privately financed alternative to the public subsidy battles that often accompany major sports venues. But Chicago City Hall’s latest proposal shows taxpayers will still have plenty invested in the surrounding project. Mayor Brandon Johnson asked the City Council on Wednesday to approve a $425 million tax increment financing package tied to infrastructure improvements at The 78, the long-stalled 62-acre megadevelopment site along the South Branch of the Chicago River. The Chicago Sun-Times reported that the subsidy would help unlock Mansueto’s planned $750 million, 22,000-seat soccer stadium […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Chicago Mayor Johnson proposes $425M TIF for The 78 megadevelopment (62-acre South Loop site).
TIF: $199M public realm, $104M roads/streetscape, $34M Metra, $30M river wall; funds underground parking garage.
Subsidy level nearly matches 2017 TIF approval ($450M); unlocks $750M, 22K-seat soccer stadium by Joe Mansueto.
stories.summary: Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson introduced a $425 million tax increment financing (TIF) package to support The 78, a long-stalled 62-acre megadevelopment on the South Branch of the Chicago River. The subsidy is nearly equivalent to the 2017 TIF approval ($450M) and is primarily driven by a planned 1,200-space underground parking garage and $750 million, 22,000-seat soccer stadium. The TIF allocation includes $199 million for public structures/plazas, $104 million for roads/streetscape, $34 million for Metra improvements, and $30 million for river wall work.
stories.impact: TIF reaffirmation at scale signals Chicago's continued commitment to South Loop megaproject delivery, which will anchor multifamily demand via job creation, destination retail, and civic amenities. However, the 10+ year timeline to full buildout and the heavy reliance on public infrastructure subsidy to overcome topographic/transit constraints raises questions about absorption pace and shadow supply once The 78 begins delivering units. Developers should assess tenant/demographic capture in surrounding neighborhoods and plan competing projects strategically to avoid saturation during The 78's buildout phase.

stories.title: Global Holdings Wraps Work on 99 Park Avenue Repositioning
stories.excerpt: Global Holdings, the international real estate development and investment firm led by chairman and founder Eyal Ofer, has completed its $30-million strategic repositioning of 99 Park Ave., a 600,000-square-foot Class A office tower near Grand Central Terminal. Designed in partnership with national architecture and interior design firm Vocon, the program reimagines the building’s arrival experience while honoring the building’s Art Deco heritage. “The repositioning of 99 Park Avenue fully integrates design and functionality, creating an environment that brings a dynamic and unmatched experience to tenants,” said Chris Roth, COO and head of office asset management, Global Holdings. “Building on successful repositionings across our portfolio, 99 Park Avenue was designed to evoke a strong sense of arrival, with interiors that feel energetic and sophisticated.” 99 Park Ave. is currently 97% leased. Recent leases have included wealth management firm Cerity Partners and real estate company Southern Land Company. The post Global Holdings Wraps Work on 99 Park Avenue Repositioning appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Global Holdings completes $30M repositioning of 99 Park Avenue (600K sf office tower near Grand Central).
Building 97% leased; recent tenants include Cerity Partners wealth management and Southern Land Company.
Design emphasizes arrival experience, Art Deco heritage, and dynamic/sophisticated interiors.
stories.summary: Global Holdings, led by Eyal Ofer, completed a $30 million strategic repositioning of 99 Park Avenue, a 600,000-square-foot Class A office tower near Grand Central Terminal in Manhattan. The renovation, designed in partnership with Vocon, reimagines the building's arrival and interior experience while honoring its Art Deco architecture. The tower is 97% leased, with recent deals from wealth management firm Cerity Partners and real estate company Southern Land Company.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily. Office repositioning (especially near transit hubs) may signal broader demand for premium NYC workplace real estate, but does not materially affect apartment supply, demand, or underwriting. However, Manhattan office stabilization can support high-income renter retention and willingness-to-pay for luxury apartments in CBD/near-CBD locations.

stories.title: MBK Opens Murrieta Rentals Following Strong Demand in Pre-Leasing Phase
stories.excerpt: Developer MBK Rental Living has opened Vintage Farms, a 15-acre community featuring 330 apartments in Murrieta. The community, now open and leasing with immediate move-ins available, has experienced early demand during its pre-leasing phase. “In today’s competitive housing landscape, differentiation matters,” said Ken McCarren, president of MBK Rental Living. “Vintage Farms reflects our commitment to creating communities with modern amenities that feel both elevated and firmly connected to their surroundings. Our goal was to design a community where the locale dotted with hillside views and outdoor gathering areas is integrated into the living environment and foundational to the resident experience.” Along with a suite of amenities intended to support multiple modes of living, Vintage Farms also incorporates the company’s ‘With Our World’ sustainability program. Elements include EV charging stations, energy-efficient LED lighting and Energy Star appliances, low or no VOC materials, bicycle racks, and a weekly recycling program. The post MBK Opens Murrieta Rentals Following Strong Demand in Pre-Leasing Phase appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: MBK Rental Living opens Vintage Farms, 330-unit apartment community in Murrieta, California.
Pre-leasing phase showed strong early demand; community fully operational with immediate move-ins available.
Development incorporates sustainability (EV charging, Energy Star appliances, low-VOC materials, recycling program).
stories.summary: MBK Rental Living has opened Vintage Farms, a 330-unit, 15-acre apartment community in Murrieta, California, with immediate lease-up and move-ins available. The project experienced strong demand during pre-leasing and is designed with modern amenities and views, complemented by a 'With Our World' sustainability program featuring EV infrastructure, energy-efficient systems, and environmental practices.
stories.impact: Successful pre-leasing and immediate absorption in Murrieta (Inland Empire/Southern California) signals sustained demand for suburban apartments in the region. The early lease-up and emphasis on sustainability/EV infrastructure reflect market preferences for environmentally conscious communities in growth-stage suburban markets. No market-wide implications unless part of broader Inland Empire supply expansion that could signal rent growth inflection or saturation.

stories.title: Union Investment, Nuveen Sell Upper East Side Retail
stories.excerpt: Union Investment and Nuveen Real Estate have sold their 1511 Third Ave. retail property on Manhattan’s Upper East Side to San Francisco-based Stockbridge. Terms of the sale were not disclosed. Union Investment acquired the retail property in 2016 as part of a closed joint venture deal with Nuveen Real Estate (then TH Real Estate) for its open-ended real estate fund Unilmmo: Global, which held a 49% stake. The fully leased property spans approximately 17,500 square feet over four floors plus a basement. The main portion of the ground floor and basement is leased Gap. The upper floors are occupied by luxury fitness company Equinox. “In a transaction market still characterized by limited transaction activity and high U.S. interest rates, we took the opportunity to sell the retail property,” said Kseniya Merritt, SVP and head of retail investments North America at Union Investment. “The exit is being made for strategic portfolio reasons and improves the fund’s liquidity position.” The CBRE New York retail team, lead by Doug Middleton and Jack Stillwagon, brokered the sale. The post Union Investment, Nuveen Sell Upper East Side Retail appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Union Investment/Nuveen sell 1511 Third Ave. Upper East Side retail property to Stockbridge (San Francisco).
17.5K sf, fully leased (Gap ground floor/basement, Equinox upper floors); sold for liquidity/strategic exit.
Transaction reflects 'limited transaction activity and high U.S. interest rates' environment for retail.
stories.summary: Union Investment and Nuveen Real Estate sold their fully leased retail property at 1511 Third Avenue on Manhattan's Upper East Side to San Francisco-based Stockbridge. The 17,500-square-foot asset (four floors plus basement) is occupied by Gap on the ground floor/basement and luxury fitness company Equinox on upper floors. The exit, terms undisclosed, was made for strategic portfolio reasons and to improve fund liquidity amid elevated interest rates and limited transaction activity.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily. Retail property sales (especially luxury/fitness) reflect broader CRE capital constraints and repositioning, but do not signal apartment demand or supply shifts. However, the sale of an Equinox-anchored property may indicate retail operators' cost pressure and potential rent reductions, which could affect mixed-use apartment/retail development pro formas in Manhattan.

stories.title: Inside litigation surrounding Amrit Ocean Resort condo-hotel
stories.excerpt: In December 2023, David Stern closed on his $1.8 million purchase of a unit at Amrit Ocean Resort, an oceanfront condo-hotel on Singer Island, a barrier island north of Palm Beach.  The following year, he found himself in the courts, suing the developer and managers of the LLCs that built the two-tower project on a nearly 7-acre plot of land at 3100 North Ocean Drive in Riviera Beach. His lawsuit hinged on the Florida statute governing condo associations and turnover of the associations’ boards. Stern alleged the developer failed to hand over control to the unit owners in a timely […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Amrit Ocean Resort condo-hotel litigation on Singer Island reflects shoddy construction, broken promises, board turnover disputes.
Unit owner purchased Dec 2023 for $1.8M; joined 36+ owners alleging unit quality and amenity deficiencies.
Broader South Florida trend: condo construction litigation rising as delayed projects complete.
stories.summary: Unit owners at Amrit Ocean Resort, a completed two-tower oceanfront condo-hotel on Singer Island (Riviera Beach), have pursued litigation alleging shoddy construction, amenity deficiencies, and developer failure to timely hand over control to the unit owners' association. One buyer who closed in December 2023 for $1.8 million joined approximately 36 other unhappy owners in raising complaints. The case represents one of several disputes against developer Dilip Barot's entities, reflecting broader South Florida trend of rising condo construction litigation as delayed projects deliver.
stories.impact: Rising condo construction litigation in South Florida signals reputational/delivery risk for developer brands and potential headwinds for condo presales in the market. However, this is a condo-hotel (mixed-use hospitality/residential) rather than pure multifamily rental; regulatory and liability frameworks differ. Multifamily rental developers should monitor condo litigation to understand buyer/owner sentiment and avoid similar construction/service quality issues if considering alternative tenure models (co-living, co-ownership platforms). No direct impact on multifamily apartment demand or supply.

stories.title: Hawkins Way Capital Drops $28M on 81 East Third Street Apartments, Student Housing
stories.excerpt: Beverly Hills-based real estate investment company Hawkins Way Capital has acquired a property comprising student housing and market-rate apartments at 81 East Third Street, Commercial Observer has learned. The $28 million acquisition in the East Village closed Wednesday, according to a source familiar with the deal. The sale comes just days after CO reported that Hawkins purchased the Upper West Side residence hall of AMDA College of the Performing Arts at 117 West 70th Street for $80 million. The 13-story building at 81 East Third Street, between First and Second avenues, spans 36,047 square feet and contains 45 residential units, according to Department of Finance records. Roughly two-thirds of the building is occupied by students at the New York Conservatory for Dramatic Arts, which uses the site as a dormitory. A school brochure for the property advertises suite-style dorms with a laundry room and common areas. The student housing portion of the building will remain intact, according to a source, but the fate of the apartments is unclear. Hawkins did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The seller entity, 81 East 3 Street Realty, is linked to the Lokshin family, a local developer and landlord, as well as landlord Simon Fouladian. The entity redeveloped the building into student and rental housing in 2005, drawing the ire of its East Village neighbors over the building’s height. BKREA‘s Bob Knakal and Ana Barrie represented both sides of the sale. Spokespeople for BKREA and the sellers could not be reached for comment. Hawkins Way, which reports $3 billion in assets under management, has undertaken a series of student housing deals over the base year. In August 2025, the firm sold a former DoubleTree by Hilton hotel at 569 Lexington Avenue to the City University of New York for $125.6 million. The month before that, it acquired the 492-room student housing tower at 99 Washington Street in the Financial District. The firm also owns a 122-
bullets: Hawkins Way Capital acquires 81 East Third Street (NYC) for $28M; 45 units (2/3 student housing, 1/3 market-rate).
Building is 13-story, 36K sf; 30 units serve New York Conservatory for Dramatic Arts dormitory.
Follows Hawkins' $80M AMDA College residence hall purchase at 117 West 70th Street (Upper West Side).
stories.summary: Beverly Hills-based Hawkins Way Capital acquired a 13-story, 36,047-square-foot property at 81 East Third Street in Manhattan's East Village for $28 million, comprising 45 residential units. Roughly two-thirds of the units serve as student housing for the New York Conservatory for Dramatic Arts; the fate of the market-rate apartments is unclear. The deal follows Hawkins' recent $80 million acquisition of the Upper West Side residence hall for AMDA College of the Performing Arts.
stories.impact: Hawkins Way's aggressive student housing/dormitory acquisition strategy signals belief in long-term NYU-adjacent market stability and recurring institutional lease income. However, student housing is a separate asset class with lower yield, longer leases, and demographic headwinds (declining college enrollment in some regions). This capital deployment does not compete directly with standard multifamily development but may indicate institutional investor flight from market-rate apartments in favor of lower-volatility student leasing. Monitor for broader investor shift away from market-rate rentals in tight, high-cost markets like NYC.

stories.title: Warsh’s First Fed Meeting as Chair Produces a Result Many in CRE Expected
stories.excerpt: President Donald Trump nominated Kevin Warsh as Federal Reserve chairman in the hope of lowering interest rates, but the first Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting under his leadership signaled a continued higher-for-longer environment. The central bank, in a unanimous 12-0 vote Wednesday, kept its benchmark interest rate at between 3.5 percent and 3.75 percent for the fourth straight meeting, citing inflation uncertainty tied to the war in Iran. The Fed also released an updated quarterly “dot plot” matrix of individual committees that removed prior forecasts of one rate cut by the end of this year and signaled possible rate hikes. The updated, more hawkish FOMC projections, which Warsh opted to not participate in, is now showing a federal funds rate of 3.8 percent at the end of 2026, up from 3.4 percent estimated in March, with nine of the 18 members signaling a near-term hike. “Economic activity is expanding at a solid pace despite elevated uncertainty that owes in part to conflict in the Middle East,” Warsh said in prepared remarks to open his post-meeting press conference. “Productivity growth and capital investment are both strong, job gains have kept pace with the workforce, and the unemployment rate has changed little.” The unanimous Fed decision Wednesday marked a stark contrast to an 8-4 vote on April 29 to maintain interest rates, a vote that marked the highest level of dissent since October 1992. The FOMC board includes former Fed chair Jerome Powell, who opted to remain as a governor and is eligible to stay in the post through January 2028. Warsh said he is forming five task forces that will determine the Fed’s use and reliance on existing data and the central bank’s inflation framework. He also announced a preference to remove “forward guidance” formerly used by the FOMC to shape future monetary policy decisions. Jay Neveloff, partner and chair of U.S. real estate at HSF Kramer, said Warsh may alter some of the ways the Fed examines data s
bullets: Federal Reserve (Chair Kevin Warsh) holds rates 3.5%-3.75% unchanged; signals 'higher for longer.'
Updated FOMC projections: federal funds rate 3.8% by end-2026 (vs. 3.4% in March); 9/18 members signal near-term hike.
Rate-cut expectations removed from 2025 forecast; inflation concerns tied to Middle East conflict cited.
stories.summary: The Federal Reserve, under newly appointed Chair Kevin Warsh, held the benchmark interest rate unchanged at 3.5–3.75% at its first FOMC meeting, signaling a "higher for longer" stance. Updated quarterly dot-plot projections removed prior forecasts of rate cuts in 2025 and raised the expected federal funds rate to 3.8% by end-2026 (from 3.4% estimated in March). Nine of 18 committee members signaled possible near-term rate hikes, citing inflation uncertainty and Middle East conflict risks.
stories.impact: Extended higher rates will tighten multifamily development economics across all regions. Cap rates may compress modestly if debt yields remain sticky, but construction financing costs will remain elevated, reducing development feasibility at lower-yielding infill/urban sites. Refinance pressure will intensify on stabilized assets with near-term maturity dates. Watch for acceleration of sponsor equity dry-ups, deal-flow compression, and selective market focus by developers toward rent-growth markets (Sunbelt, tech hubs) with pricing power sufficient to offset financing headwinds. This signals a prolonged period of capital discipline and selective development.

stories.title: Kushner, Cain pay $43M for Edgewater site amid apartment rent-growth predictions
stories.excerpt: Cain and Kushner paid $43.1 million for a site in Edgewater where they’ve been planning a 364-unit luxury apartment tower.  The firms bought the 1.5-acre property at 614 and 720 Northeast 27th Street from an entity tied to a joint venture between Miami-based OKO Group and Cain.  London-based Cain, led by Jonathan Goldstein, and New York-based Kushner, headed by Laurent Morali and Nicole Kushner Meyer, have been planning the 40-story project since April. BDT & MSD Partners provided a $42 million loan for the development, with construction expected to start late next year, The Real Deal previously reported.  The property […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Kushner/Cain JV pays $43.1M for 1.5-acre Edgewater, Miami site for 364-unit luxury tower.
BDT & MSD Partners fund $42M loan; construction expected late 2025; first project under new partnership.
South Florida apartment supply down 7.8% vs. 3-year avg; modest rent growth predicted; Cain/Kushner rank 2nd in investor intentions.
stories.summary: A joint venture between Cain and Kushner paid $43.1 million for a 1.5-acre site at 614 and 720 Northeast 27th Street in Edgewater, Miami, where they are planning a 40-story, 364-unit luxury apartment tower. BDT & MSD Partners provided a $42 million construction loan; construction is slated to begin in late 2025. This is the first development under the newly formed Cain-Kushner South Florida joint venture. South Florida's apartment supply is down 7.8% versus the three-year average, with strong demand driven by elevated homeownership costs and modest rent growth predicted for next year.
stories.impact: Kushner-Cain partnership entry signals top-tier developer confidence in South Florida multifamily fundamentals and ability to source trophy sites. Constrained supply (-7.8% vs. historical avg) and strong investor appetite (ranked 2nd in CBRE 2025 intentions) suggest rents are poised to accelerate and development margins remain attractive despite high financing costs. The $43M land basis for a 364-unit luxury tower implies ~$118K/unit land cost and likely $70K-80K/unit total soft/hard costs, supportive of $2,200+ rents needed to achieve mid-4s cap rates. Watch for ripple effect of top-tier partnership entry drawing additional sponsor capital to Edgewater and Miami rental market, potentially tightening available land and bidding up remaining acquisition targets.

stories.title: City Invests $1B for New Affordable Housing Preservation Program
stories.excerpt: The New York City Department of Housing Preservation and Development (HPD) has put into action what it calls the Supportive Preservation Program (SPP), designed to provide greater support for and increase the preservation of affordable housing across the city’s five boroughs, the HPD announced Tuesday. Supportive housing around the city will see an investment of $1 billion from HPD, with a portion of that funding specifically dedicated to the preservation of New York City’s supportive housing supply, The Real Deal reported. The program is meant to specifically support units that provide services to people in danger of homelessness and facing other complex housing issues. That translates to more than 30,000 units. “What we’re announcing here today is a commitment to supportive housing in New York City and the New Yorkers who rely on it,” Patrick Love, deputy commissioner of development at HPD, said on Tuesday announcing the news. “The supportive housing model is a model that works.” Projects eligible for the SPP must be existing supportive housing projects in New York City and must have existing social service contracts administered by the city, according to the announcement. Through the SPP, the HPD will be able to grant tax exemptions, provide below-market loans and modify existing loans. Details about the timeline of HPD’s investment are unclear. “Supportive housing plays a critical role in our city, providing stability and services to New Yorkers in need. I am proud to be launching the new Supportive Preservation Program to ensure that providers and residents are able to succeed for many years to come,” Leila Bozorg, deputy mayor for housing and planning, said in a statement. “This program will be an important part of our holistic approach to provide and protect the housing that New Yorkers rely on.” Since entering office, Mayor Zohran Mamdani has made it his administration’s mission to supply New York City with more affordable housing. In May, the mayor an
bullets: NYC Department of Housing Preservation & Development announces $1B Supportive Preservation Program.
Investment targets preservation of 30K+ supportive housing units across five boroughs.
Program funds services for populations in danger of homelessness and facing complex housing issues.
stories.summary: The New York City Department of Housing Preservation and Development (HPD) launched the Supportive Preservation Program (SPP), committing $1 billion to preserve affordable supportive housing across NYC's five boroughs. The program is focused on units that provide services to people in danger of homelessness and facing complex housing challenges, targeting preservation of more than 30,000 units.
stories.impact: NYC's $1B supportive housing preservation commitment signals city-level focus on preventing stock loss rather than purely new production. This may reduce available sites for market-rate multifamily development as best urban/transit-adjacent sites are steered toward preservation/supportive use under public subsidy. The policy shift suggests stricter affordability requirements for new development and potential headwinds for luxury rental projects in regulated markets. Multifamily developers should anticipate higher affordability/set-aside obligations in NYC pro formas and consider value-add repositioning of older stock toward hybrid workforce/market-rate models aligned with city policy priorities.
